chapterSummary.title: Thousands Around Us
chapterSummary.content: This chapter introduces students to the world of large numbers -- from thousands to ten-thousands. Students learn about place value for numbers up to 5 digits and understand that the value of a digit depends on its position. They practise reading and writing numbers in standard form, expanded form, and number names. The chapter also covers comparing and ordering numbers using the symbols >, <, and =. Finally, students learn to estimate and round numbers, connecting maths to real-life situations like populations, distances, and prices.
keyPoints: Place values from right to left: Ones, Tens, Hundreds, Thousands, Ten-Thousands
The smallest 4-digit number is 1,000 and the largest is 9,999
Numbers can be written in standard form, expanded form, and number names
To compare numbers, first count the digits, then compare from the left
Ascending order goes from smallest to largest; descending order goes from largest to smallest
Rounding rule: 5 or more round up, less than 5 round down
learningObjectives: Understand place value for numbers up to 5 digits
Read and write 4-digit and 5-digit numbers in words and numerals
Write numbers in expanded form and convert back to standard form
Compare two large numbers using >, <, and =
Arrange numbers in ascending and descending order
Estimate and round numbers to the nearest thousand

curatedNotes.title: Place Value Chart for 5-Digit Numbers
curatedNotes.content:
Use this chart to understand any number up to 5 digits:

Ten-Thousands | Thousands | Hundreds | Tens | Ones

Example: 47,835
4 = 40,000 (Ten-Thousands)
7 = 7,000 (Thousands)
8 = 800 (Hundreds)
3 = 30 (Tens)
5 = 5 (Ones)

The value gets 10 times bigger as you move one place to the left!

curatedNotes.title: How to Compare Big Numbers
curatedNotes.content:
Step 1: Count the digits. The number with more digits is always bigger. A 5-digit number is always greater than a 4-digit number.

Step 2: If both numbers have the same number of digits, start comparing from the leftmost digit. The first digit that is different tells you which number is bigger.

Example: 45,231 vs 45,198. First two digits (4,5) are the same. Third digit: 2 vs 1. Since 2 > 1, we know 45,231 > 45,198.

curatedNotes.title: Rounding Made Easy
curatedNotes.content:
To round a number, look at the digit just to the right of the place you are rounding to.

Rounding 6,743 to the nearest thousand: Look at the hundreds digit (7). Since 7 is 5 or more, round up: 7,000.

Rounding 6,743 to the nearest hundred: Look at the tens digit (4). Since 4 is less than 5, round down: 6,700.

Remember: 5 or more -- go up! Less than 5 -- stay down!
